cards.title: Thread Lifecycle & ExecutorService
cards.explanation: A Java thread moves through six states: NEW (created, not started), RUNNABLE (executing or ready to execute), BLOCKED (waiting for a monitor lock), WAITING (indefinitely waiting for another thread), TIMED_WAITING (waiting with a timeout), and TERMINATED (finished). Creating threads directly via new Thread() is discouraged in production — prefer ExecutorService which pools and reuses threads, reducing creation overhead and providing lifecycle management.
cards.whyItMatters: Raw thread creation is expensive (~1MB stack per thread by default). ExecutorService lets you control concurrency level, handle exceptions via Future, and gracefully shut down — all critical for production services.
cards.gotcha: Calling future.get() without a timeout blocks the calling thread indefinitely if the task hangs. Always use future.get(timeout, TimeUnit.SECONDS) in production code.

cards.title: synchronized vs ReentrantLock
cards.explanation: The synchronized keyword acquires the intrinsic (monitor) lock of an object, automatically releasing it when the block exits. ReentrantLock from java.util.concurrent.locks provides more flexibility: tryLock() attempts to acquire without blocking, lockInterruptibly() can be cancelled, and a fairness parameter ensures FIFO ordering. Both support re-entrance (a thread that already holds the lock can acquire it again). ReentrantLock must be manually unlocked — always in a finally block.
cards.whyItMatters: synchronized is fine for simple mutual exclusion. ReentrantLock is needed when you require timed or interruptible lock acquisition, fairness guarantees, or multiple Condition objects for fine-grained wait/notify.
cards.gotcha: Acquiring locks in inconsistent order across threads causes deadlock. Always acquire multiple locks in the same canonical order (e.g., by object identity: System.identityHashCode). The transfer() example above is vulnerable if two threads call it with swapped from/to.

cards.title: volatile & the Java Memory Model
cards.explanation: The Java Memory Model allows threads to cache variables in CPU registers or caches, making changes invisible to other threads. Declaring a field volatile guarantees visibility: every write is flushed to main memory and every read fetches from main memory. Volatile does NOT guarantee atomicity — volatile int count++ is still a read-modify-write race. Volatile establishes a happens-before relationship: a write to a volatile field happens-before every subsequent read of that same field.
cards.whyItMatters: Without volatile, the JIT compiler or CPU can reorder memory operations in ways that break multi-threaded correctness even when the logic looks right. Volatile is the minimal tool for flag variables and the fix for double-checked locking.
cards.gotcha: volatile long and volatile double are atomic on 64-bit JVMs but NOT guaranteed atomic by the JLS on 32-bit JVMs. For guaranteed atomic compound operations (increment, compare-and-swap) use AtomicInteger, AtomicLong, or AtomicReference instead.

cards.title: CompletableFuture Basics
cards.explanation: CompletableFuture is Java 8's async pipeline API. supplyAsync runs a Supplier on the ForkJoinPool (or a custom executor), returning a future. thenApply transforms the result (like map). thenCompose chains another async operation (like flatMap — avoids CompletableFuture<CompletableFuture<T>>). exceptionally handles errors. allOf waits for all futures; anyOf waits for the first.
cards.whyItMatters: CompletableFuture replaces callback hell and Future.get() blocking. It enables non-blocking async pipelines and parallel fan-out (allOf) that are readable and composable.
cards.gotcha: supplyAsync without a custom executor uses ForkJoinPool.commonPool(). Submitting blocking I/O to this pool starves it and hurts parallel stream performance across the JVM. Always provide a dedicated executor for I/O-bound tasks.

cards.title: thenCompose vs thenApply, handle vs exceptionally
cards.explanation: thenApply is synchronous transformation (result → new result). thenCompose accepts a function that returns another CompletableFuture, flattening the nesting — use it when the next step is itself async. handle receives both the result and the exception (one will be null), allowing you to recover or rethrow — it runs whether or not the stage completed exceptionally, unlike exceptionally which only runs on failure.
cards.whyItMatters: Confusing thenApply and thenCompose produces CompletableFuture<CompletableFuture<T>>, which compiles but is never what you want. Choosing handle vs exceptionally correctly determines whether recovery logic applies in the success path.
cards.gotcha: handle and whenComplete always run even if the previous stage was cancelled. If you need to propagate cancellation, check whether the exception is a CancellationException before recovering.

cards.title: Virtual Threads (Java 21)
cards.explanation: Virtual threads (Project Loom, JEP 444) are lightweight threads managed by the JVM, not the OS. Millions can exist simultaneously because they are mounted onto a small pool of OS 'carrier' threads and unmounted when blocking (I/O, sleep, synchronized). This makes blocking code as scalable as reactive/async code without the callback complexity. Virtual threads are ideal for high-concurrency I/O-bound workloads (web servers, DB calls).
cards.whyItMatters: Virtual threads eliminate the 'one thread per request' scalability ceiling without requiring reactive programming. A Tomcat server with virtual threads handles the same load as a Netty reactive server with far simpler, sequential-looking code.
cards.gotcha: Virtual threads are NOT faster for CPU-bound work — they only help when threads spend time blocked on I/O. For CPU-bound tasks, you still want a bounded thread pool sized to available processors. Also avoid ThreadLocal caches with virtual threads (potentially millions of threads = millions of cached objects).
